mission.title: Install and Configure NATS
mission.description: NATS is a high-performance messaging system designed for cloud-native applications. It provides a lightweight and scalable solution for communication between services and devices. Key trade-offs include simplicity and speed versus advanced features found in more complex messaging systems.

steps.title: Add NATS Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official NATS Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add nats https://nats-io.github.io/k8s

helm repo update

```

> This repository contains the NATS Helm charts.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install NATS Server
steps.description:
This command installs the NATS server using Helm with the specified version.

```bash

helm install nats nats/nats --version 2.11.0 --namespace nats --create-namespace

```

> Installs NATS in a new namespace called 'nats'.

steps.title: Create NATS Configuration
steps.description:
This step creates a custom configuration for NATS to define its behavior.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: nats-config
  namespace: nats
data:
  nats-server.conf: |
    port: 4222
    http_port: 8222
EOF

```

> Creates a ConfigMap with a basic NATS configuration.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: nats-config
  namespace: nats
data:
  nats-server.conf: |
    port: 4222
    http_port: 8222
EOF

steps.title: Verify NATS Deployment
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the NATS pods to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n nats

```

> You should see NATS pods in a Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall NATS Server
uninstall.description:
This command removes the NATS server and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all data stored in NATS.

```bash

helm uninstall nats --namespace nats

kubectl delete namespace nats

```

> Removes the NATS release from the cluster.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade NATS Server
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your configuration and data. This step will upgrade NATS to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n nats -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade nats nats/nats --version 2.11.0 --namespace nats

kubectl uncordon <pod-name>

```

> Drains the NATS pods before upgrading.
> Upgrades NATS to the specified version.
> Uncordon the pods after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get pods -n nats -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: NATS pods are in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the NATS server configuration.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the NATS pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n nats' for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to connect to NATS server
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or firewall rules may be blocking access.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the correct ports (4222, 8222) are open and accessible.

troubleshooting.title: NATS server not responding
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The server may be overloaded or misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Review the NATS configuration and resource limits set for the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm install fails with 'chart not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The NATS Helm repository may not be added or updated.

**Fix:**
Run 'helm repo add nats https://nats-io.github.io/k8s' and 'helm repo update'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for JetStream, improved performance metrics.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v2.10, review the JetStream documentation for configuration changes.

resolution.summary: You should see NATS pods running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n nats' showing status as 'Running'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
